text: The Market for Extortion and Protection Felipe Balmaceda 1 and Martin Castillo-Quintana 2 1 Institute for Economic Policy-Andrés Bello University 2 Harris School of Public Policy-University of Chicago January 13, 2025 (Work in progress)
States worldwide face significant challenges in maintaining the Weberian concept of monopolizing the legitimate use of violence and ensuring the protection of individuals within their territories.
The presence of non-state groups engaging in protection and extortion thwarts efforts to provide security, enforce property rights, address underdevelopment, and foster economic growth.

This paper develops a model to analyze the dynamics of strategic competition in extortion and protection provision under the shadow of the state, examining the impact of various state policies on the outcomes of this violent competition.
Latin American states face significant challenges in maintaining the Weberian concept of monopolizing the legitimate use of violence and protecting individuals within their territories.
In Port-au-Prince, major gang coalitions have taken control of critical parts of the country, running kidnapping enterprises and charging informal fees for people and cargo being transported 1.
In Santiago, gangs engage in small-scale warfare over the control of downtown areas to extort and racketeer businesses 2.
In Mexico, the count of self-defense groups has reached a significant number, indicating a lack of state control 3.

Medellin has widespread evidence of "combos" providing order in exchange for the “vacuna.” 4 In Rio de Janeiro, paramilitaries and various drug-trafficking organizations compete to control the favelas 5.
In El Salvador, the government has taken draconian measures to combat MS-13 and Barrio 18, which specialize in extorting the local population 6.
These examples illustrate the severe difficulties Latin American states face in asserting their authority and ensuring the safety of their inhabitants.

The prevalence of non-state groups specializing in illicit activities such as protection, racketeering, and extortion poses a core policy challenge when providing security to individuals and businesses, enforcing property rights, addressing underdevelopment and fostering growth.
Current economic development theories underscore the necessity of a state that can effectively secure property rights and ensure the safety of its citizens and businesses.

This project aims to enhance our theoretical understanding of the mechanisms when multiple non-state groups violently compete to extort the population and how this affects extortion’s intensity under varying state policies.

By examining the interactions between these groups and state authorities, we seek to shed light on how these dynamics impede economic development and explore potential policy solutions to mitigate their impact.
Most scholarly attention on these topics has concentrated on when criminal groups vie for control over other illicit markets such as drugs, oil, and gambling.
However, the competition for territorial control to extort the population operates under different dynamics.

This project has two primary objectives: first, to develop a game https://www.nytimes.com/2024/03/07/world/americas/haiti-gangs-explainer.html 2 https://www.mega.cl/programas/mucho-gusto/mega-investiga/169132-bandas-extranjeras-disputaterritorial-santiago.html 3 https://www.diariolasamericas.com/america-latina/mexico-grupos-autodefensa-vencer-o-ceder-laviolencia-n5346407 4 https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/3bncz 5 https://brazilian.report/society/2024/04/17/drug-traffickers-militias-rio-territory/ 6 https://www.dw.com/es/el-salvador-suma-78000-presos-en-guerra-contra-pandillas/a-68676454 theoretical model that elucidates the dynamics of competition in the market of extortion and protection under the shadow of the state, and second, to complement the existing empirical literature on this subject.
We aim to provide a deeper understanding of the mechanisms driving extortion and extortion-related violence and inform more effective policy responses.
Let’s draw and compare two existing empirical papers that study this phenomenon to motivate some of the challenges underlying this research.

Brown et al. study the extortion fees charged by Salvadorian gangs before and after a truce, they find that in times of peace, they charge higher fees to the truck drivers that dispatch merchandise to their territories.
In contrast, Magaloni et al. find that territorial competition among Mexican criminal groups increases extortion.
These apparently contradictory findings suggest the presence of distinct mechanisms influencing extortion, which may exert opposing effects.
Why would competition increase extortion in Mexico while reducing it in El Salvador?

Contribution to the literature While there is limited theoretical work on how competition for the market of protection influences extortion efforts over the population and a lack of theory on how various state policies might affect this relationship, this paper aims to fill this gap by drawing insights and parallels from existing literature on state formation, the economics of crime, the sociology of crime and criminal politics.
Frameworks from state formation serve as a crucial first building block for our work.

Since Tilly, the seminal notion that state-making resembles organized crime allows us to draw parallels between the violent competition among criminal groups and among states over the control of extortion of a given circumscribed population.
States competed for territory, leading to divisions where each surviving state emerged as the monopoly provider of protection and taxation.

Following the typologies developed by Olson, the outcome of a conflict between criminal groups determines which group will adopt the behavior of a roving bandit and which will establish itself as a stationary bandit.
Another strand of literature on which this paper will build is the economics of crime.

Constructing on the frameworks of Schelling and Becker, we will assume that when criminal groups focus on extorting civilian populations, they engage in rational cost-benefit analysis and strategically respond not only to state policies but also to civil responses and the actions of other criminal groups.

When non-state criminal groups extort businesses and communities, it creates inefficiency because extortion costs can be passed along to customers and victims, particularly if the demand in the victim’s market is inelastic.
The project will build upon an important piece of literature where specialists in violence compete to get control of who extorts individuals or businesses.

Skaperdas and Syropoulos; Moselle and Polak; Skaperdas; Mehlum et al.; Hafer; Garfinkel and Skaperdas; Brown et al. develop models where specialists of violence directly or indirectly compete over the right to extort economic activity.

This class of models’ main findings is that gang competition efficiency follows a U-shaped relationship with asymmetry in endowments, and the availability of more destructive weapons increases conflict inefficiency.

Additionally, a higher return to banditry leads to an increase in the number of bandits and a decrease in welfare for both peasants and bandits, with monopolized crime leading to varying societal outcomes compared to disorganized crime— Moselle and Polak finds it results in better welfare outcomes.
In contrast, Brown et al. finds the opposite.
Furthermore, gang competition heightens violence, whereas collusion among gangs leads to higher levels of extortion.
McGuire and Olson; Konrad and Skaperdas provide another class of models that focuses on the interaction between victims and the perpetrators of extortion.

Their main findings are that monopoly bandit-state can enhance economic productivity by balancing taxation with public goods provision, highlighting the trade-off between immediate revenue extraction and long-term economic outcomes and that a gang with monopoly extortion engages in no extortion when victims are few, but as the number of victims increases, it becomes profitable to engage in extortion, and there will be expected losses from violence in larger populations.
Finally, Blattman et al. provide the only existing game theoretical framework where gangs respond to state policies.
In their model, the state and a gang offer protection services without directly conflicting with each other, functioning as strategic substitutes.
They also conducted a field experiment in Medellin, enhancing the security provision of civilian state presence.
This led to varying outcomes depending on the initial level of state legitimacy.

A fundamental approach of this project is to integrate, in a tractable manner, the diverse mechanisms captured by existing models within the literature and to complement the existing empirical literature by capturing the scope conditions under which violent competition and extortion fees are positively and negatively associated.
Additionally, it will provide a benchmark for the role of state policies in mediating this violence and extortion.
The model will be dynamic to allow the formation of peace agreements as an endogenous outcome.

This integrated approach will enable a deeper analysis of the conditions under which violent actors adjust their behaviors in response to state interventions, contributing to the literature to formulate policies to reduce violence and foster economic development.
